cards.grammarNote:
To express pain: **[body part] يؤلمني** (yu'limuni) = '[body part] hurts me' (masculine nouns) / **تؤلمني** (tu'limuni) (feminine nouns).
Alternatively: **عندي ألم في** [body part] (indi alam fi...) = I have pain in [body part].

**شَعر** (shaʿr, hair) is a collective noun — it refers to hair as a mass. For a single strand: **شَعرَة** (shaʿra). Root **ش-ع-ر** (sh-ʿ-r, to feel/be aware/have hair). Same root as **شِعر** (shiʿr, poetry) and **شَاعِر** (shāʿir, poet) — the overlap of sensitivity and hair.
**لَدَيهَا** (ladayhā) = she has — possessive preposition **لَدَى** + feminine suffix **ـهَا** (hā, her). **شَعر طَوِيل** — indefinite noun + adjective, no article.

**وَجه** (wajh, face) from root **و-ج-ه** (w-j-h, to face/direct). **وَجَّهَ** (wajjaha, to direct — Form II), **اِتَّجَهَ** (ittajaha, to head toward — Form VIII), **وِجهَة** (wijha, direction/viewpoint), **وُجِهَ إِليهِ** (to be directed at).
Possessive: **وَجهُكَ** (wajhuka, your face m.) — suffix **ـكَ** (-ka). Imperative **اِغسِل** (ighsil, wash!) from root **غ-س-ل** (gh-s-l, to wash) — Form I imperative.

**عَين** (ʿayn, eye) is feminine. Dual: **عَينَان** (ʿaynān, two eyes). The phrase **عَينَاهُ زَرقَاوَان** uses the dual subject + dual adjective. **زَرقَاء** (zarqāʾ, blue, feminine) → dual **زَرقَاوَان** (zarqāwān).
Note: **عَين** also means 'spring of water' and 'essence/self' — a beautifully polysemous root. The letter **ع** itself is named *ʿayn* because it was associated with the shape of an eye in ancient Semitic scripts.

**أُذن** (udhn, ear) is feminine. Dual: **أُذُنَان** (udhunan, two ears). The verb **تُؤلِمُني** uses the feminine form **تُـ** because the ear is feminine — **يُؤلِمُني** would be used for masculine body parts.
Root **أ-ذ-ن** (ʾ-dh-n, to permit/give ear): **أَذِنَ** (adhina, to allow), **أَذَان** (adhān, call to prayer — the announcement one 'gives ear' to). **مَأذُون** (maʾdhūn, authorized person).

**أَنف** (anf, nose) is masculine. Root **أ-ن-ف** (ʾ-n-f, nose/pride/reluctance). **أَنِفَ** (anifa, to disdain — literally 'to be nose-proud'), **أَنَفَة** (anafa, pride/disdain). The same root gives us pride idioms: **شَامِخ الأَنف** (nose held high = arrogant).
The adjective **بَارِد** (bārid, cold) is masculine and agrees with **أَنف** (masculine). Compare: **رَقبَتِي بَارِدَة** (my neck is cold) — feminine **ة** because neck is feminine.

**فَم** (fam, mouth) — an unusual word: it is one of the few Arabic nouns with no clear trilateral root. In possessive constructions the **م** changes: **فَمِي** (famī, my mouth), **فَمُكَ** (famuka, your mouth), but **فُو** (fū) in construct: **فُو الإِنسَان** (fū al-insān, the human mouth) — a classical form.
**اِفتَح** (iftaḥ, open! — imperative Form I) from root **ف-ت-ح** (f-t-ḥ, to open). **فَتح** also means 'conquest/opening' — Saladin's conquest of Jerusalem was called **الفَتح**.

**شَفة** (shafa, lip) — tā marbūṭa **ة** signals a feminine noun. Dual: **شَفَتَان** (shafatān, two lips). In the phrase, **شَفَتَاهَا** = her two lips (dual possessive). The color adjective **حَمرَاء** (ḥamrāʾ, red, feminine) → dual **حَمرَاوَان** (ḥamrāwān).
Note: tā marbūṭa (**ة**) appears at the end of many feminine nouns and adjectives. It is pronounced **ة** (a) in pause but **ت** (t) when followed by another word.

**سِنّ** (sinn, tooth) is feminine. Dual: **سِنَّان** (sinnān, two teeth). Broken plural: **أَسنَان** (asnān, teeth — pattern **أَفعَال**). Root **س-ن-ن** (s-n-n, to sharpen/set). **سِنّ** also means 'age/year': **كَم سِنُّهُ؟** = how old is he?
The verb **تُؤلِمُني** is feminine to agree with **سِنّ** (feminine). The phrase **أَسنَان** (teeth, plural) is used in dental contexts: **طَبِيب الأَسنَان** (dentist — literally 'doctor of teeth').

**لِسَان** (lisān, tongue/language) is masculine. Root **ل-س-ن** (l-s-n, tongue/language). The word covers both the physical tongue and 'language/tongue' in a linguistic sense: **اللِّسَان العَرَبِيّ** = the Arabic tongue/language. Plural: **أَلسِنة** (alsina, tongues — broken plural **أَفعِلة**).
**أَخرِج** (akhrij, take out! — imperative Form IV) from root **خ-ر-ج** (kh-r-j, to exit). Form IV imperative = causative command: 'cause to come out.'

**رَقَبة** (raqaba, neck) — tā marbūṭa **ة** marks it as feminine. Root **ر-ق-ب** (r-q-b, to watch/guard). **رَقِيب** (raqīb, watchman/guard — **فَعِيل**), **مُرَاقَبة** (murāqaba, surveillance/observation — Form III verbal noun). The neck was linked to watching/looking out.
**مُتَصَلِّبة** (mutaṣalliba, stiff, feminine) — Form V active participle from root **ص-ل-ب** (ṣ-l-b, to be hard/rigid). Feminine agrees with **رَقَبة** (feminine).

**ذِرَاع** (dhirāʿ, arm/forearm) is both masculine and feminine — classical Arabic allows both genders. Dual: **ذِرَاعَان** (dhirāʿān, two arms). Root **ذ-ر-ع** (dh-r-ʿ, forearm/measure). **ذِرَاع** was also a unit of length (cubit = forearm length).
**كَسَرتُ** (kasartu, I broke) from root **ك-س-ر** (k-s-r, to break). Past tense first-person singular ends in **ـتُ** (-tu): **ذَهَبتُ** (I went), **أَكَلتُ** (I ate).

Arabic has a dual form for pairs: **يد** (yad = hand) → **يدان** (yadan = two hands).
In **اغسل يديك** (ighsil yadayk), **يديك** is the dual possessive.
Dual forms apply across paired body parts: **عينان** (two eyes), **رجلان** (two legs).

**إِصبَع** (iṣbaʿ, finger/toe) can be masculine or feminine. Broken plural: **أَصَابِع** (aṣābiʿ — pattern **أَفَاعِل**). Root **ص-ب-ع** (ṣ-b-ʿ, finger). **إِصبَع القَدَم** = toe (literally 'finger of the foot').
**قَطَعَ** (qaṭaʿa, to cut) from root **ق-ط-ع** (q-ṭ-ʿ, to cut/sever). A productive root: **قَطِيعة** (qaṭīʿa, estrangement), **اِنقِطَاع** (inqiṭāʿ, interruption — Form VII verbal noun).

**صَدر** (ṣadr, chest/breast) is masculine. Root **ص-د-ر** (ṣ-d-r, to issue from/chest). **صَدَرَ** (ṣadara, to issue/be issued), **صُدُور** (ṣudūr, chests — broken plural **فُعُول**), **مَصدَر** (maṣdar, source/verbal noun — the 'issuing point' of action).
**عِندَهُ** (ʿindahu, he has — literally 'at him') + **أَلَم** (ʾalam, pain): **عِندَهُ أَلَم فِي الصَّدر** = he has pain in the chest. **الصَّدر** — sun letter **ص** causes assimilation.

**ظَهر** (ẓahr, back) is masculine. Root **ظ-ه-ر** (ẓ-h-r, to appear/back). **ظَهَرَ** (ẓahara, to appear/become visible), **ظَاهِرة** (ẓāhira, phenomenon — 'something that appears'), **ظُهُور** (ẓuhūr, appearance/back — verbal noun). The verb **يُؤلِمُني** is masculine (**يُـ** prefix) to agree with **ظَهر** (masculine).
**أَسفَل الظَّهر** (asfal aẓ-ẓahr, lower back) — **أَسفَل** (asfal, lower/bottom) + definite **الظَّهر** in an iḍāfa chain. Sun letter **ظ**: *aẓ-ẓahr*.

**قَلب** (qalb, heart) is masculine. Root **ق-ل-ب** (q-l-b, to turn/flip). **قَلَبَ** (qalaba, to flip/overturn), **اِنقَلَبَ** (inqalaba, to be overturned/revolution — Form VII), **تَقَلُّب** (taqallub, fluctuation/turning). The heart was called **قَلب** because it 'turns over' (beats/changes states).
**يَدُقُّ** (yaduqqu, it beats) from root **د-ق-ق** (d-q-q, to beat/knock/be precise). **دَقَّة** (daqqa, a beat/knock), **دَقِيق** (daqīq, precise/minute — also flour!).

**بَطن** (baṭn, stomach/belly) is masculine. Root **ب-ط-ن** (b-ṭ-n, inside/belly). **بَاطِن** (bāṭin, inner/hidden — **فَاعِل**), **بَاطِنِيَّة** (bāṭiniyya, internal medicine), **بُطُون** (buṭūn, bellies — broken plural **فُعُول**). The inner/hidden sense of the root extends to: **عِلم البَاطِن** = esoteric knowledge.
Verb **يُؤلِمُني** is masculine (prefix **يُـ**) agreeing with **بَطن** (masculine).

**عَضَلة** (ʿaḍala, muscle) — tā marbūṭa **ة** marks it as feminine. Root **ع-ض-ل** (ʿ-ḍ-l, muscle/difficulty). **عَضَّلَ** (ʿaḍḍala, to make difficult — Form II), **مُعضِلة** (muʿḍila, dilemma/difficult problem — literally 'a muscular/knotty thing'). Plural: **عَضَلَات** (ʿaḍalāt, muscles — sound feminine plural).
**شَدَدتُ** (shadadtu, I pulled) from root **ش-د-د** (sh-d-d, to pull/tighten). Geminated root: **شَدَّ** → past 1st person **شَدَدتُ**.

**خَصر** (khaṣr, waist) is masculine. Root **خ-ص-ر** (kh-ṣ-r, to narrow/be slim). **خَاصِرة** (khāṣira, flank/side — **فَاعِلة** pattern), **اِختَصَرَ** (ikhtaṣara, to abbreviate/shorten — Form VIII), **مُختَصَر** (mukhtaṣar, summary/abbreviation). The 'narrowing' sense connects waist, abbreviation, and the flank.
**رَفِيع** (rafīʿ, slim/slender/noble — **فَعِيل** pattern) from root **ر-ف-ع** (r-f-ʿ, to raise/elevate). Masculine because it agrees with **خَصر** (masculine).

**جِلد** (jild, skin/hide/leather) is masculine. Root **ج-ل-د** (j-l-d, skin/to flog). **جِلدِيَّة** (jildiyya, dermatology — nisba adjective), **جِلدِيّ** (jildī, dermatological), **مُجَلَّد** (mujallad, bound book — 'covered in skin/leather').
**نَاعِم** (nāʿim, soft/smooth — **فَاعِل** pattern) from root **ن-ع-م** (n-ʿ-m, to be soft/pleasant). **نِعمَة** (niʿma, blessing/grace) and **نَعَم** (naʿam, yes/domestic animals) share this root.

**دَم** (dam, blood) is masculine. Root **د-م-و/ي** (d-m-w, blood). **دَمَوِيّ** (damawī, bloody/blood-related), **ضَغط الدَّم** (blood pressure). Note: **دَم** is a defective noun — its full form would be **دَمَو** or **دَمَي**, but only the base form **دَم** is used.
**تَبَرَّعَ بِـ** (tabarraʿa bi-, to donate — Form V): **بَرَّعَ** (barraʿa, to give generously) → **تَبَرَّعَ** (tabarraʿa, to donate/volunteer). **تَبَرُّع** (tabarruʿ, donation). The **بِـ** preposition introduces what is donated.

**دِمَاغ** (dimāgh, brain) is masculine. MSA also uses **مُخّ** (mukhkh, brain/marrow) from root **م-خ-خ** (m-kh-kh). **دِمَاغ** from root **د-م-غ** (d-m-gh, brain/to crush). **دَمَغَ** (damagha, to crush/stamp), **دَامِغ** (dāmigh, crushing/decisive — **فَاعِل**).
**مُعَقَّد** (muʿaqqad, complex/complicated) — Form II passive participle from root **ع-ق-د** (ʿ-q-d, to knot/contract). Literally 'knotted.' **عَقد** (ʿaqd, knot/contract), **مَعقُود** (contracted).

**جُمجُمة** (jumjuma, skull) — a quadriliteral root **ج-م-ج-م** showing reduplication. Feminine (tā marbūṭa **ة**). Plural: **جَمَاجِم** (jamājim — broken plural **فَعَالِل**).
**تَحمِي** (taḥmī, it protects — feminine verb prefix **تَـ**) from root **ح-م-ي** (ḥ-m-y, to protect). The verb is feminine because its subject **الجُمجُمة** is feminine. Defective verb: present tense ends in **ي** → **يَحمِي / تَحمِي** (he/she protects).

**صَدر** (ṣadr) serves as both 'chest' (general) and 'breast' — context distinguishes. The word is masculine despite often referring to female anatomy. Root **ص-د-ر** (ṣ-d-r, to issue/chest). **شَعَرَت** (shaʿarat) — past feminine: root **ش-ع-ر** + feminine suffix **ـت** (-at).
**بِأَلَمٍ** (bi-alamin, with/of pain) — the **بِـ** preposition after **شَعَرَ** is obligatory. **صَدرِهَا** = her chest — possessive suffix **ـهَا** (hā, her/its).
cards.englishPhrase: She felt pain in her chest.

cards.grammarNote:
**سَاق** (sāq, leg/shin) is feminine. Dual: **سَاقَان** (sāqān, two legs). Root **س-و-ق** (s-w-q, to drive/leg). **سَوَّقَ** (sawwaqa, to market — Form II), **سُوق** (sūq, market — 'the place one drives/goes to'). All from the same root!
**مُتعَبة** (mutʿaba, tired, feminine) agrees with **سَاق** (feminine). The predicate adjective must match the noun: feminine noun → feminine adjective.

**قَدَم** (qadam, foot) is feminine. Dual: **قَدَمَان** (qadamān). Possessive dual: **قَدَمَايَ** (qadamāya, my two feet). Root **ق-د-م** (q-d-m, foot/to come forward): **قَدِيم** (qadīm, old/ancient), **مُقَدِّمة** (muqaddima, introduction — 'what comes first'), **قَدَّمَ** (qaddama, to present — Form II).
**تُؤلِمَانِّي** — dual feminine verb (prefix **تُـ** + dual suffix **ـَان**) + object suffix **ـِّي** (me). This is the dual verb form matching dual subject **قَدَمَاي**.

**إِصبَع القَدَم** (iṣbaʿ al-qadam) = toe, literally 'finger of the foot' — an iḍāfa chain. Arabic uses the same word for finger and toe, distinguished only by adding **القَدَم** (the foot). Plural: **أَصَابِع القَدَم** (aṣābiʿ al-qadam, toes).
**اِصطَدَمَ بِـ** (iṣṭadama bi-, to collide with/stub against — Form VIII): root **ص-د-م** (ṣ-d-m, to crash/collide). Form VIII inserts **ت** after the first radical: **ص** + **ط** (assimilation of ت after ص) → **اِصطَدَمَ**.

**مِفصَل** (mifṣal, joint) — **مِفعَل** instrument-noun pattern from root **ف-ص-ل** (f-ṣ-l, to separate/join). Literally 'the place of separation/articulation.' **فَصَلَ** (faṣala, to separate), **فَصل** (faṣl, chapter/separation/season), **تَفصِيل** (tafṣīl, detail — 'separating out').
Broken plural: **مَفَاصِل** (mafāṣil — **مَفَاعِل**). **مُتَصَلِّبَة** (mutaṣalliba, stiff, plural feminine) agrees with **مَفَاصِل** — non-human plurals take feminine singular agreement.

**نَفَس** (nafas, breath) is masculine. Root **ن-ف-س** (n-f-s, breath/self/soul). One of the richest roots: **نَفس** = breath AND self/soul (same word, different vowels in context). **نَفسِي** = myself / my soul / my breath. **تَنَفَّسَ** (tanaffasa, to breathe — Form V), **اِنفُس** (unfus, souls — plural).
**اِحبِس** (iḥbis, hold! — imperative Form I) from root **ح-ب-س** (ḥ-b-s, to imprison/hold back). **حَبس** (ḥabs, imprisonment/holding).

**يَرَى** (yarā, he sees) — defective verb from root **ر-أ-ي** (r-ʾ-y, to see/think). The hamza drops in many forms: present **يَرَى**, past **رَأَى** (raʾā). **رُؤيَة** (ruʾya, sight/vision — verbal noun), **رَأي** (raʾy, opinion — 'what one sees').
**أَستَطِيع** (astaṭīʿ, I can/am able) — Form X of root **ط-و-ع** (ṭ-w-ʿ, to obey/be capable). Form X = to seek the ability. **بِوُضُوح** (bi-wuḍūḥ, clearly) — root **و-ض-ح** (w-ḍ-ḥ, to be clear).

**يَسمَع** (yasmaʿ, he hears) from root **س-م-ع** (s-m-ʿ, to hear). **سَمِعَ** (samiʿa, to hear — Form I), **سَمَاع** (samāʿ, hearing/listening to music), **اِستَمَعَ** (istamaʿa, to listen attentively — Form X). Distinguish: **سَمِعَ** = passive hearing; **اِستَمَعَ** = active listening.
**لَا أَسمَعُكَ** — **لَا** + present tense = present negation: 'I don't/can't hear you.' The suffix **ـكَ** (ka) = you (masculine). For a woman: **لَا أَسمَعُكِ** (-ki).

**يَشُمّ** (yashumm, he smells) from root **ش-م-م** (sh-m-m, to smell). A geminate root: the last two radicals are the same (**م-م**). Present: **يَشُمّ** (yashumm), past: **شَمَّ** (shamma). **رَائِحة** (rāʾiḥa, smell/scent — **فَاعِلة** feminine active participle) from root **ر-و-ح** (r-w-ḥ, breath/rest).
**الزُّهُور** (az-zuhūr, flowers) — broken plural of **زَهرَة** (zahra, flower) — pattern **فُعُول**. Sun letter **ز** causes assimilation: *az-zuhūr*.

**يَتَذَوَّق** (yatadhawwaq, he tastes) — Form V of root **ذ-و-ق** (dh-w-q, to taste). Form V (**تَفَعَّلَ**): **ذَاقَ** (dhāqa, to taste briefly) → **تَذَوَّقَ** (tadhawwaqa, to taste carefully/appreciate). **ذَوق** (dhawq, taste/aesthetic sense), **ذَوَّاق** (dhawwāq, one with refined taste — **فَعَّال** intensive).
**الحِسَاء** (al-ḥisāʾ, soup/broth) from root **ح-س-و** (ḥ-s-w, to sip). The imperative **تَذَوَّق** drops the initial **تَـ** prefix: Form V imperative.

**يَلمَس** (yalmas, he touches) from root **ل-م-س** (l-m-s, to touch). **لَمسَة** (lamsa, a touch/stroke), **مَلمَس** (malmas, texture/feel — **مَفعَل** place/instrument noun), **لَامَسَ** (lāmasa, to touch/be in contact with — Form III).
**لَا تَلمَس** = don't touch — prohibition: **لَا** + jussive present tense. **ذلِك** (dhālika, that) is the demonstrative for distant/removed objects. Nearby: **هَذَا** (hādhā, this).

**يَتَنَفَّس** (yatanaffas, he breathes) — Form V of root **ن-ف-س** (n-f-s, breath/soul). Form V implies a continuous/repeated action: **نَفَّسَ** (naffasa, to relieve) → **تَنَفَّسَ** (tanaffasa, to breathe steadily). **تَنَفُّس** (tanaffus, breathing — verbal noun).
**بِبُطء** (bi-buṭʾ, slowly) — **بِـ** + **بُطء** (buṭʾ, slowness) from root **ب-ط-أ** (b-ṭ-ʾ, to be slow). This **بِـ + noun** pattern creates adverbial phrases: **بِسُرعَة** (quickly), **بِهُدُوء** (calmly).

**يَنحَنِي** (yanḥanī, he bends) — Form VII of root **ح-ن-و/ي** (ḥ-n-w, to bend/curve). Form VII (**اِنفَعَلَ**) is reflexive/passive: **حَنَا** (ḥanā, to bend something) → **اِنحَنَى** (inḥanā, to bend oneself/bow). **حَنِين** (ḥanīn, longing/yearning — literally 'a bending of the soul toward something').
**رُكبَتَيكَ** (rukbatayka, your two knees) — dual possessive: **رُكبَتَان** (dual) → **رُكبَتَي** (construct) + **ـكَ** (your).

**يَمُدّ** (yamudd, he stretches/extends) from root **م-د-د** (m-d-d, to extend). Geminate root: **مَدَّ** (madda, to stretch). **مَدَّة** (madda, a stretch/duration), **إِمدَاد** (imdād, supply/reinforcement — Form IV verbal noun), **مَدِينة** (madīna, city — literally 'the extended place').
**ذِرَاعَيكَ** (dhirāʿayka, your two arms) — dual possessive. Imperative **مُدَّ** = stretch! — geminate verb: the doubled **د** is written with shadda: مُدَّ.

**كَتِف** (katif, shoulder) can be masculine or feminine — both genders are used in classical Arabic. Root **ك-ت-ف** (k-t-f, shoulder/to bind the arms). Dual: **كَتِفَان** (katifān). Broken plural: **أَكتَاف** (aktāf — pattern **أَفعَال**).
**يُؤلِمُني** (yuʾlimunī, hurts me) — masculine verb form agreeing with **كَتِف** when treated as masculine. The suffix **ـنِي** (-nī) = me (object pronoun attached to verb).

**رُكبَة** (rukba, knee) — tā marbūṭa **ة** marks it as feminine. Dual: **رُكبَتَان** (rukbatān, two knees). Root **ر-ك-ب** (r-k-b, to ride/kneel). **رَكَبَ** (rakaba, to kneel/ride), **رَاكِب** (rākib, rider — **فَاعِل**), **مَركَبة** (markaba, vehicle — **مَفعَلة** pattern).
**أَصَبتُ فِي** (aṣabtu fī, I hurt/injured in) from root **ص-و-ب** (ṣ-w-b, to hit/be correct): **أَصَابَ** (aṣāba, to hit/injure — Form IV). Past tense 1st person: **أَصَبتُ**.

**مِرفَق** (mirfaq, elbow) — **مِفعَل** instrument/place pattern from root **ر-ف-ق** (r-f-q, to accompany/be gentle). Literally 'the place of companionship' (the elbow bends toward the body, or the arm bends to accompany a companion). **رِفق** (rifq, gentleness), **رَفِيق** (rafīq, companion/comrade — **فَعِيل**), **مُرَافِق** (murāfiq, escort/accompanying).
Broken plural: **مَرَافِق** (marāfiq — **مَفَاعِل**). Masculine noun: verb **يُؤلِمُنِي** agrees.

**يَثنِي** (yathnī, he flexes/bends) from root **ث-ن-ي** (th-n-y, to fold/bend/praise). **ثَنَى** (thanā, to fold/bend), **مَثنَى** (mathnā, the act of doubling — related to **اِثنَان**, two), **اِستَثنَاء** (isthnaʾ, exception — 'folding out').
**عَضَلَاتِكَ** (ʿaḍalātika, your muscles) — sound feminine plural **ـَات** + possessive **ـكَ** (your). Non-human plurals are grammatically treated as feminine singular in verb agreement, but plural forms are used with possessives normally.

**يَرمُش** (yarumsh, he blinks) from root **ر-م-ش** (r-m-sh, to blink/eyelash). **رَمش** (ramsh, eyelash/blink) — the same word covers both the action and the eyelash. In colloquial Arabic (Egyptian/Levantine) **يَرمُش** is common; MSA also uses **يَرفُّ** (yaruffu, from **ر-ف-ف**, to flutter).
**لَا تَرمُش** = don't blink — **لَا** + jussive (the vowel of the final consonant often changes or drops in jussive). A useful construction in medical or photography contexts.

**يَتَعَرَّق** (yataʿarraq, he sweats) — Form V of root **ع-ر-ق** (ʿ-r-q, sweat/vein). Form V = to undergo the process: **عَرِقَ** (ʿariqa, to sweat — Form I stative) or **تَعَرَّقَ** (taʿarraqa, to perspire). **عَرَق** (ʿaraq, sweat — noun; also the anise liquor), **عِرق** (ʿirq, vein/nerve/root — same root!).
**عِندَمَا** (ʿindamā, when — subordinating conjunction) = at the time that.

**يَنزِف** (yanzif, it/he bleeds) from root **ن-ز-ف** (n-z-f, to bleed/drain). **نَزَفَ** (nazafa, to bleed), **نَزِيف** (nazīf, hemorrhage — **فَعِيل** noun/adjective), **نَازِف** (nāzif, bleeding — **فَاعِل**).
**الجُرح** (al-jurḥ, the wound) is masculine; hence **يَنزِف** uses masculine prefix **يَـ**. Root **ج-ر-ح** (j-r-ḥ, to wound): **جَرَحَ** (jaraḥa, to wound), **جَرَّاح** (jarrāḥ, surgeon — **فَعَّال** intensive: 'one who wounds/cuts expertly').

**يَشعُر** (yashʿur, he feels) from root **ش-ع-ر** (sh-ʿ-r, to feel/perceive). Always used with **بِـ** when feeling an emotion: **شَعَرَ بِـ** = to feel [noun]. In medical contexts, **كيفَ تَشعُر؟** = how do you feel? Note: physical sensation often uses **يُؤلِمُني** (hurts me) or **عِندِي** (I have [symptom]) instead.
**كَيفَ** (kayfa, how) introduces the question. No verb 'to be' is needed in the question.

**يُفَكِّر** (yufakkir, he thinks) — Form II of root **ف-ك-ر** (f-k-r, to think). Form II intensifies: **فَكَرَ** (fakara, to think once) → **فَكَّرَ** (fakkara, to think carefully/reflect). **فِكر** (fikr, thought), **فِكرَة** (fikra, idea), **تَفكِير** (tafkīr, thinking — Form II verbal noun).
**بِمَ** (bi-mā) = about what? — **بِـ** (about) + **مَا** (what). The **ما** loses its final **ا** before another word in classical style. **تُفَكِّر فِي** or **تُفَكِّر بِـ** = to think about.

**يَتَحَرَّك** (yataḥarrak, he moves) — Form V of root **ح-ر-ك** (ḥ-r-k, to move). Form V is reflexive: **حَرَّكَ** (ḥarraka, to move something — Form II) → **تَحَرَّكَ** (taḥarraka, to move oneself). **تَحرِيك** (taḥrīk, moving something — Form II verbal noun), **حَرَكَة** (ḥaraka, movement/vowel mark in Arabic script).
**لَا أَستَطِيع + verbal noun** = I can't [do action]. **تَحرِيك** is the verbal noun used as direct object here.

**ذُو عِظَام بَارِزَة** = having prominent bones. **ذُو** (dhū, possessor of) is one of the 'five nouns' (*al-asmāʾ al-khamsa*) that change form: **ذُو** (nominative), **ذَا** (accusative), **ذِي** (genitive). Dual: **ذَوَا** (dhawā, nominative dual).
**عِظَام** (ʿiẓām, bones) — broken plural of **عَظم** (ʿaẓm, bone) — pattern **فِعَال**. **بَارِزَة** (bāriza, prominent — **فَاعِلة**) from root **ب-ر-ز** (b-r-z, to protrude/appear).

**كَثِيف الشَّعر** (kathīf ash-shaʿr) = dense-haired/hairy — an iḍāfa adjective phrase: **كَثِيف** (dense — **فَعِيل**) + **الشَّعر** (the hair). Root **ك-ث-ف** (k-th-f, to be dense/thick). **كَثَافة** (kathāfa, density), **تَكَاثَفَ** (takāthafa, to condense — Form VI).
Dual adjective: **كَثِيفَا الشَّعر** (kathīfā ash-shaʿr) in the nominative dual, as it modifies the dual subject **ذِرَاعَان**. Dual adjective endings: nominative **ـَا**, accusative/genitive **ـَي**.

**نَاعِم** (nāʿim, soft/smooth) — **فَاعِل** active participle from root **ن-ع-م** (n-ʿ-m, to be gentle/soft). Feminine: **نَاعِمة** (nāʿima). Related words: **نِعمَة** (niʿma, blessing), **نَعَم** (naʿam, yes — agreement/acceptance), **أَنعَم** (anʿama, to bestow blessings — Form IV).
**النَّسِيج** (an-nasīj, the tissue/fabric) — sun letter **ن** causes assimilation. **نَسَجَ** (nasaja, to weave), **نَسِيج** (nasīj, fabric/tissue — **فَعِيل**) from root **ن-س-ج**.

**مُؤلِم** (muʾlim, painful/sore) — Form IV active participle from root **أ-ل-م** (ʾ-l-m, to hurt). **أَلَمَ** → **أَلَّمَ** / **أَوجَعَ** = to cause pain. Feminine: **مُؤلِمة** (muʾlima).
**عَضَلَاتِي مُؤلِمَة** — non-human plurals (here **عَضَلَات**, muscles) take a **feminine singular** predicate adjective in classical Arabic: **مُؤلِمَة** (not **مُؤلِمُون**). This is the famous rule: 'broken and sound feminine plurals of non-humans are treated as feminine singular.'

**أَيسَر** (aysar, left — masculine superlative-form adjective) from root **ي-س-ر** (y-s-r, to be easy/left). **يُسرَى** (yusr ā, left, feminine) / **أَيسَر** (aysar, left, masculine). Ironically the root **ي-س-ر** means 'easy' — the left hand was the 'easier' in ancient usage.
Feminine form **اليُسرَى** (al-yusr ā) is used here because **رُكبَة** (knee) is feminine. Compare: **اليَمِين** / **اليُمنَى** (right, masculine/feminine) from root **ي-م-ن** (y-m-n, right/blessing).

**أَيمَن** (aymayn, right — masculine) / **اليُمنَى** (al-yumnā, the right, feminine) from root **ي-م-ن** (y-m-n, right/blessing/south). **يَمِين** (yamīn, right side/oath), **يُمنَى** (yumnā, right — feminine form), **يَمَن** (Yaman, Yemen — the land to the right when facing Mecca from Arabia).
The phrase uses **اليُمنَى** (feminine) because **ذِرَاع** (arm) is feminine. The adjective follows the noun and takes the definite article to match: **ذِرَاعِي اليُمنَى** = my right arm.

**عُلوِيّ** (ʿulwī, upper/elevated) — nisba adjective from **عُلوّ** (ʿuluww, height/elevation) from root **ع-ل-و** (ʿ-l-w, to be high). **عَالٍ** (ʿālin, high — **فَاعِل**), **أَعلَى** (aʿlā, higher/highest), **عَلِيّ** (ʿAlī, the name meaning 'exalted').
**الجُزء** (al-juzʾ, the part) from root **ج-ز-أ** (j-z-ʾ, to divide). **جُزء** = part/portion; plural **أَجزَاء** (ajzāʾ — pattern **أَفعَال**).

**سُفلِيّ** (suflī, lower) — nisba adjective from **سُفل** (sufl, bottom/below) from root **س-ف-ل** (s-f-l, to be low). **أَسفَل** (asfal, lower/bottom — elative **أَفعَل** pattern), **سُفلِيَّات** (sufliyyāt, lower things).
**أَسفَل الظَّهر** (asfal aẓ-ẓahr) = the lower back — iḍāfa: **أَسفَل** (the lower part) + **الظَّهر** (the back). Sun letter **ظ** in **الظَّهر**: *aẓ-ẓahr*. Note **عِندَهُ أَلَم** = he has pain (possession construction).

**جِسم** (jism, body) is masculine. Root **ج-س-م** (j-s-m, body/to be corporeal). **جَسَد** (jasad, body — especially with spiritual connotation), **مُجَسَّم** (mujassam, three-dimensional/embodied — Form II passive participle). Arabic has two body words: **جِسم** (physical body as a whole) and **جَسَد** (body esp. as vessel of the soul).
**البَشَرِيّ** (al-basharī, human — nisba adjective) from **بَشَر** (bashar, human/mankind): **بَشَر** is the collective noun for humanity.

**بَشَرِيّ** (basharī, human) — nisba adjective from **بَشَر** (bashar, mankind). The **ـِيّ** (ī) suffix creates adjectives from nouns: **مِصرِيّ** (Egyptian), **عَرَبِيّ** (Arabic/Arab), **بَشَرِيّ** (human).
**عِلم الأَحيَاء** (ʿilm al-aḥyāʾ, biology) = science of living things — iḍāfa: **عِلم** (science) + **الأَحيَاء** (the living beings — broken plural of **حَيّ**, living, from root **ح-ي-ي**). **إِنَّهُ** (innahu) = indeed it is — emphatic particle **إِنَّ** + pronoun suffix **ـهُ**.
cards.phrase: إنه جزء من علم الأحياء البشري.
